bull.b: Large, structured collaborations with Takeda (and amended Lilly deal) provide milestone-driven non-dilutive funding and de-risk development. These agreements align economics with clinical progress, sustain R&D financing over multiple years and strengthen commercialization optionality for lead programs.

A conservative debt profile gives AC Immune financial flexibility and lowers near-term solvency risk while it pursues costly clinical programs. Low leverage supports the ability to fund downside scenarios, access debt markets if needed, and negotiate partnership terms without immediate refinancing pressure.

Advancing multiple clinical-stage assets (ACI-24, ACI-7104, NLRP3 inhibitor, Morphomer tau) with favorable safety and biomarker signals demonstrates R&D productivity and de-risks the portfolio. Consistent clinical milestones can trigger partner payments and sustainable development pathways over the medium term.

bear.b: Large, recurring operating losses reflect the company's development-stage cost structure and mean continued reliance on external funding. Persistent deficits increase dilution risk and pressure governance choices, limiting capacity to scale programs without material financing or further partnerships.

Irregular collaboration and milestone timings drive revenue swings, undermining predictability of internal funding. This volatility constrains long-term planning, forces reactive cost management and heightens dependence on milestone-triggered partner receipts to sustain clinical development.

A shrinking equity base reduces the company's capital buffer against clinical setbacks and limits non-dilutive financing capacity. With limited internal reserves, adverse trial outcomes or delayed milestones could accelerate dilution or necessitate unfavorable financing, raising long-term capital risk.
